We’re incredibly honored to share that CHP Truckee has been recognized with the 2025 AAMVA Social Media Award.

AAMVA (American Association of Motor Vehicle Administrators) represents driver licensing and traffic safety agencies across the U.S. and Canada. This award recognizes agencies using social media in a positive, impactful way to promote traffic safety and serve our communities.

To our followers, THANK YOU! 

Whether you’re here for the light-hearted Donner Summit humor, the chain control updates, the storm warnings, or the real-time road info… you’re the reason we do this. Our goal has always been simple.. mix a little personality with a whole lot of safety.

We’ll continue to keep you informed, keep you prepared, and keep pushing out messages that help protect everyone traveling through the Truckee area. Our mission remains the same, Safety, Service, and Security for all.

We hope to see you out and about in Truckee… just maybe not featured in one of our social media posts 😉

California Highway Patrol 
American Association of Motor Vehicle Administrators

This place is about to blow… 💨❄️

The storm’s over, but the work isn’t. Caltrans crews are still out across the Truckee/Tahoe area clearing and widening lanes, you’ll see plows and blowers throughout the region, so give them space and slow it down.

With all this fresh powder, expect a major increase in traffic throughout the Truckee/Tahoe Basin as visitors head up to enjoy the snow. Plan ahead and be prepared for significant delays all weekend.

Our goal is for everyone to get to and from their destination safely.

Caltrans District 3 
#CHPTruckee #CaltransPuttingInWork #TahoeTraffic
#WeekendWarriors

If your vehicle looks like it’s carrying its own snowbank…. We’re going to notice ❄️👀

We’ve been pulling drivers to the side at chain control just to clear the snow stacked on their roofs. Once you get to highway speeds, that snow becomes an airborne ice sheet. The driver behind you definitely didn’t sign up for that.

Take an extra few minutes before you roll out and completely clear your roof, hood, and lights. It keeps everyone safer and helps us keep the freeway open.

#CHPTruckee #WinterDriving #ClearTheRoof #DonnerSummit

It’s electric ⚡️
….until it’s not.

Freezing temps and whiteout conditions are undefeated up here. Cold weather drains batteries faster than you think. If you’re rolling over the Summit, make sure your charge level matches your confidence level.

⚠️ I- 80 remains closed in both directions over Donner Summit due to severe weather and dangerous driving conditions.

Charge up. Slow down. Carry chains.
And don’t test Mother Nature.

If you’re wondering why the freeway is closed.. this is why. Donner Summit is not in the mood today. 

High winds are creating full whiteout conditions across the summit. Snow is blowing right back onto the roadway, and visibility is near zero.

This isn’t inconvenient weather.
This is unsafe travel. If you don’t absolutely need to be out, don’t be.
Donner doesn’t care about your schedule. We’ll reopen when it’s safe, not before.

You don’t have to be a meteorologist to know this calm won’t last… 

A strong storm rolls in late tonight and sticks around into early next week, bringing heavy snow, gusty winds, and near whiteout conditions at times. In other words, this is the kind of weather that can quickly turn I-80 into a parking lot. If your travel isn’t essential, consider waiting it out. If you do need to travel, be prepared, plan for delays, and carry chains. Give yourself extra time and adjust to the conditions.

Respect the mountain and let’s make good decisions…

#CHPTruckee #DonnersGonnaDonner #WinterDriving #MakeGoodDecisions

When your GPS says “recalculating” and you decide the railroad tracks look like a solid U-turn option…  Yeah, that’s a no. 😬

Driver missed their turn, thought the tracks were a good place to turn around, and quickly found out winter plus tracks equals, you’re going to get stuck. Thankfully no train, no injuries.. just a long night and a reminder that tracks are never a turnaround.

If you miss your turn, keep going. We promise another one exists 😅

🚓 Traffic Breaks — What You Need to Know 🚓

If you’ve ever seen a CHP patrol vehicle slowly weaving across freeway lanes, you were likely witnessing a traffic break. Officers use this maneuver to gradually slow vehicles behind them when there’s a hazard ahead—such as debris in the roadway, a stalled vehicle, or a collision blocking lanes.

During a traffic break, it’s important for drivers to stay behind the patrol vehicle and not attempt to pass. California Vehicle Code requires motorists to remain at least 300 feet back until the roadway is safe and the officer moves out of the lanes.

These controlled slowdowns help protect stranded motorists, emergency personnel, and everyone traveling on the freeway. Your patience helps keep our roadways safe.

62 years ago today, Officer Glenn W. Carlson laid down his life rather than swerve from the path of duty.

Officer Carlson was on duty when he stopped a speeding vehicle occupied by three men in a chain-controlled area on Donner Pass Rd. (Old Highway 40) west of Donner Lake in the Sierra Nevada.  Officer Carlson issued the driver a citation.

Soon after, Officer Carlson was informed the car was stolen from a used car lot in Sacramento, and he began pursuit, according to CHP records. Unknown to the 33-year-old officer, those in the vehicle were fleeing from a Bank of America robbery hours earlier in Sacramento.

The vehicle pulled over in front of Loch Leven Lodge by Donner Lake, and the men — Robert Burns, a parolee from Oregon, and fellow parolees Roger Mealman and Clifford Toycen Jr. — exited and approached Carlson.

When Carlson ordered the suspects back, Mealman shot him five times with a 9mm pistol, killing him. All three suspects were later apprehended and charged with Carlson's death.

In 2003 the SR-267 bypass in Truckee was dedicated in Officer Carlson's memory.

Officer Carlson's sacrifice will never be forgotten here in Truckee.

After 26 weeks of intense training at the CHP Academy, not one… not two… but three brand-new officers have officially joined the Truckee Area! This historic moment marks the first time in over 16 years that three officers have reported in at once straight from the academy. It is with profound sorrow that the California Highway Patrol announces the line-of-duty death of Officer Miguel Cano, ID 23555.

In the early morning hours of July 2, 2025, at approximately 0028 hours, Officer Cano, faithfully serving the West Los Angeles Area, suffered a suspected medical emergency while on patrol. Tragically, his vehicle left the roadway and collided with a tree.

Emergency personnel responded without delay, and Officer Cano was transported to Ronald Reagan UCLA Medical Center. Despite exhaustive life-saving measures, he was pronounced deceased at 0140 hours.

Officer Cano was a dedicated public servant who embodied the values of courage, commitment, and compassion. His service to the people of California will never be forgotten.

He is survived by his loving wife and devoted parents. The California Highway Patrol offers its deepest condolences to Officer Cano’s family, friends, and fellow officers. We honor the legacy of a hero who gave his all in service to others.

#CaliforniaHighwayPatrol

🚁 From whiteouts to trailouts, H-20 always shows up. ❄️➡️☀️

During winter, we’re busy assisting with snow-related emergencies, but when the snow melts, the rescues don’t stop. With the warmer weather, CHP H-20 continues to assist our Allied Agencies on rescue missions all across the Sierra. Whether it’s a lost hiker, injured biker, or backcountry mishap we’re here when things go sideways. But out here, “Expect to Self Rescue” isn’t just a phrase.. it’s reality.

Know your route, bring the right gear, and have a backup plan. Tell someone where you’re going and when you’ll be back. And please, don’t treat cell signal like a survival strategy.

Enjoy the beauty of Truckee and Tahoe this weekend but do it smart, and do it safe. 😎

CHP - Valley Division Air Operations

🗑 Trash Talkin’ with the Locals 🐻

Caught this fuzzy dude living his post-hibernation dream: 🍽️ Dumpster Lunch with a view. We politely asked him to vacate the premises… but let’s be honest, he BEARly cared. With warm weather and summer creeping in, so are the bears. And they’re not just dumpster diving... they’re checking car menus too.

🍿DO NOT leave food in your car unless you want a surprise co-pilot with no sense of personal space.
🚗 Lock those vehicle doors! Unless you want to explain to your insurance why a bear tried to hotwire your Subaru.

BEAR AWARE reminders:
🔒 Secure trash bins!
🚫 Don’t leave food out, unless you’re into wildlife dining experiences
🚪 Shut windows and doors tighter than your jeans after winter
👀 Keep a high visual horizon while driving… bears don’t wear neon vests when crossing the road.

Let’s keep our furry friends and our community safe by doing our part! Respect the wild, secure your snacks, and give bears space to just be bears.

While out and about, we don’t just assist stranded motorists, do public service, or handle the normal duties of a California Highway Patrol officer, we sometimes get the privilege of being part of something truly special.

Meet Frank, a beautiful German Shepherd with a heart full of love… and one that’s unfortunately growing tired. Frank was recently diagnosed with serious health issues, and time with him is now incredibly precious. While chatting with his dad, Ben, Officer Perez learned of one very heartfelt wish: to have Frank sit in a real police car, just once.

So today, Frank rode in style with CHP Truckee. He hung his head out the window, soaked up the sunshine, and got all the love from our team. It was an honor to help make this moment happen. We’re sending strength and love to Frank and Ben during this difficult time. Thank you for letting us be part of your journey.

Once a good boy, always a good boy! 🐾❤️🐶

Well, folks, this driver took “off-roading” to a whole new level… straight down an embankment and onto a bike path. The cause? Suspected DUI. Thankfully, the driver only sustained minor injuries, and nobody else was hurt. But this could have ended much differently.

🚔 Make good choices! plan ahead, designate a sober driver, or call a ride. Let’s keep our roads (and bike paths) safe for everyone.

🚨 SCAM ALERT 🚨

Phone scammers are posing as “AMBER Alert representatives”, offering to “register” your children. They ask for confidential info and to meet at your home. This is NOT how the AMBER Alert system works!

🔹 The California Highway Patrol (CHP) is the only agency authorized to activate AMBER Alerts—no registration is required.
🔹 Never provide personal information or answer calls from unknown or “possible scam” numbers.
🔹 If you’ve been contacted by a scammer, report it to your local law enforcement agency immediately.

Stay informed and protect yourself!

#ScamAlert #AMBERAlert #CHP #ProtectYourFamily #ReportScams #CHPWarning #StaySafe

🏔️Ski Slopes Aren’t Roadways 🚙

Late last night, CHP Truckee officers were called to an unusual scene — a white Jeep stranded on Northstar California Resort's ski slope. Turns out, this driver’s idea of a “hotel shortcut” was anything but… and a few drinks didn’t help their navigation skills.

With assistance from NorthStar’s incredible crew and their snowcat, CHP officers made their way up the slope. After investigating, the driver was arrested for suspicion of driving under the influence. Stoke meter? 0% Arrest meter? 100%

Remember: Alcohol impairs judgment and driving ability. Don’t let a poor decision turn into a costly mistake. Plan a sober ride and help keep the roads — and ski slopes — safe for everyone. NEVER DRINK AND DRIVE and leave the “first chair” to the skiers! 🚔❄️⛷️

🌲❄️ Calm Before the Storm ❄️🌲

Ahh, Truckee serenity: crisp air, a peaceful lake, and clear skies. But don’t let this peaceful scene fool you – this is the “calm before the storm” and we’re about to swap tranquility for winter driving conditions and chain control! We are expecting a winter storm starting tonight through the weekend, so if you’re planning to travel through Donner Summit tonight, now’s the time to prepare!

❄️Winter Survival Checklist:

✅ Tires – Check ‘em. Chains – Bring ‘em, because “I’ll be fine” is not a real strategy.
✅ Supplies – Pack extra snacks, water, and warm clothes. Think less “casual road trip” and more “winter camping with wheels.”
✅ Full Tank of Gas – Because your car can’t run on hope.
✅ Time – Add some. Plan extra travel time, expect delays, check the weather periodically, and don’t be the reason the traffic reports says, “Freeway closed, major delays.”

Our officers will be out ensuring safety, but a little preparation goes a long way. Drive safe, be smart, and respect the storm! 🌨️🚓

61 years ago today, Officer Glenn W. Carlson laid down his life rather than swerve from the path of duty.

Officer Carlson was on duty when he stopped a speeding vehicle occupied by three men in a chain-controlled area on Donner Pass Rd. (Old Highway 40) west of Donner Lake in the Sierra Nevada.  Officer Carlson issued the driver a citation.

Soon after, Officer Carlson was informed the car was stolen from a used car lot in Sacramento, and he began pursuit, according to CHP records. Unknown to the 33-year-old officer, those in the vehicle were fleeing from a Bank of America robbery hours earlier in Sacramento.

The vehicle pulled over in front of Loch Leven Lodge by Donner Lake, and the men — Robert Burns, a parolee from Oregon, and fellow parolees Roger Mealman and Clifford Toycen Jr. — exited and approached Carlson.

When Carlson ordered the suspects back, Mealman shot him five times with a 9mm pistol, killing him. All three suspects were later apprehended and charged with Carlson's death.

In 2003 the SR-267 bypass in Truckee was dedicated in Officer Carlson's memory.

Officer Carlson's sacrifice will never be forgotten here in Truckee.

Mountain snow is forecasted for tonight, with another storm expected to roll in on Friday. Drivers should be prepared for snow-covered roads and chain controls, and if you are reading this and googling “What is chain control and the different restrictions” - no worries, we got you, and we posted the details above. Before you travel, make sure your vehicle isn’t just Winter-Ready but Donner-Ready! Below are some tips to help you navigate this winter: 

🔹 Check Your Vehicle: Check your brakes, battery, headlights, windshield wipers, defroster, check your tire pressure when tires are cold, check the tread on your tires, carry jumper cables, and make sure you have a full tank of gas. GET THOSE CHAINS OUT!!  (Yes, the ones still tangled in your garage from last year), practice your “I totally know what I’m doing look” and prepare for some quality time with the steering wheel as you crawl over the summit at the thrilling speed of 30 mph.

🔹 Pack an Emergency Kit: Pack water, warm blankets and clothing, shovel, ice scraper, flashlight, cell phone, cell phone charger, road flares, fuel, a spare vehicle key, and extra non-perishable food.  
🏔️ Pro Tip: If you find yourself stuck on the summit, don’t panic. You’re just experiencing the legendary Donner Party vibes - minus the whole… well, you know… just carry extra snacks and keep it civilized 😊  

🔹 Drive for conditions: SLOW DOWN and do not ride your brakes! Increase your following distance between yourself and the vehicle in front of you. And we will say it again… SLOW DOWN and drive like your grandma is in the passenger seat, or enjoy your new winter hobby of snowbank parking. 

🔹 PLAN AHEAD: Periodically check for local road and weather conditions. The "Caltrans QuickMap" App is a great resource for road conditions. 

🚗 Remember:  AWD/4x4 does not always mean all wheel stop in snowy and icy conditions. Just because your truck/SUV is a beast doesn’t mean it can stop on ice. Trust us, Donner Summit doesn’t care about your vehicle’s ego—slow and steady wins this race! 🐢

Let’s work together to make this winter safe and smooth for everyone! ❄️🚔

Caltrans District 3

Snow flurries have begun over Donner Summit, officially kicking off the season of ‘Do I really need chains?’ and ‘How slow is too slow?’ Skiers are already rubbing it in, while the rest of us are just trying to remember where we stashed the ice scraper last April. Buckle up, folks—it’s winter’s soft opening!” Whether you’re a seasoned snow pro or new to the winter wonderland, we’ve got some tips to help you not end up on our blooper reel:

🔹 Tire Check: If your tires look like they belong on a skateboard, it’s time to upgrade. Winter tires, chains—heck, just duct tape some snowshoes on them if you have to…But seriously, come prepared!

🔹 Slow Your Roll: You’re not auditioning for “Fast & Furious: Frozen Drift.” Roads can get icy, and nobody wins a prize for slipping into a snowbank. Take it easy and give yourself plenty of time to get where you’re going.

🔹 Pack a Winter Survival Kit: Blankets, snacks, water…basically, pretend you’re packing for a sleepover in your car. Because let’s face it, that might happen if you’re not careful (or if you need an emergency snack break).

🔹 Stay Updated: Follow @CHP-Truckee and Caltrans District 3 for road conditions, travel advisories, and an occasional winter meme. Trust us, you don’t want to be the reason we post one.

** UPDATE August 30, 2024 at 4pm **

—DUI SOBRIETY/DRIVER LICENSE CHECKPOINT --

The Truckee office of the California Highway Patrol (CHP) will conduct a sobriety/driver license checkpoint on Friday, August 30, 2024.

The goal of the CHP is to ensure the safe passage of each and every motorist by targeting roads where there is a high frequency of intoxicated drivers.  The Placer County Sheriff’s Department will be participating in the sobriety/driver license checkpoint.  This is a combined effort to increase traffic safety within in the unincorporated area of Placer County, in the north Tahoe basin. A sobriety checkpoint is a proven and effective tool for achieving this goal.  It is designed to augment existing patrol operations.  By publicizing our efforts, we believe that we can deter motorists from drinking and driving.

Traffic volume permitting, all vehicles will be checked for drivers who are under the influence of alcohol and or drugs.  Our objective is to send a clear message to those individuals that consider mixing alcohol or drugs with driving.

California Highway Patrol celebrates 95 years of Service!

Today marks 95 years of providing the highest level of Safety, Service, and Security to the People and State of California.

The California Highway Patrol law enforcement agency has been grounded in its core values of courtesy and service since 1929. At its founding, the Department’s mission was to facilitate the safe and secure transport of persons and property upon California’s highways and to provide service to the motoring public as well as fair and unified enforcement of traffic safety laws. Since then, as the Department has adapted and evolved through every challenge and change, we continue to embrace those fundamental core values. 

The mission of the California Highway Patrol is to provide the highest level of Safety, Service, and Security. 

Join the CHP’s Legacy at https://chpmadeformore.com/

Looks like the EXTREME HEAT ADVISORY will continue throughout California this weekend and next week! We know it won’t be as hot here in Truckee as it will in CHP - Indio or CHP - Mojave, but us mountain people can’t handle the heat 🤣 Here are some safety tips to help you through the heat:

o Stay hydrated, drink water often.
o NEVER leave kids or pets in your car.
o Ensure your vehicle’s A/C is functioning before hitting the road.
o Inform someone of your travel plans and check in periodically.
o Avoid strenuous outdoor activities.
o Check on vulnerable people. 
o Follow local weather updates and heat alerts.

Have a good weekend, stay cool, and be safe!!

✨✨FLASHBACK FRIDAY! ✨✨With more storms expected this weekend, let's all remember Grogu's famous words... 

If you are planning to travel over Donner Summit this weekend, here are some tips: 

• Slow the fast down! reduce your speed and increase your following distance! Remember, Four-Wheel/AWD Does Not Mean Four Wheel Stop!
• Prepare your vehicle and check your vehicles’ equipment prior to leaving (tires, headlights, windshield wipers). 
• Make sure you carry an emergency kit, chains, extra non-perishable food, water, a shovel, ice scraper, warm clothing, and make sure your car has a full tank of gas.
• Plan ahead, expect delays, and monitor weather conditions periodically. The Caltrans QuickMap App is a great tool to check on road conditions and incidents. 
• Give plows extra room! Caltrans is working hard to clear the roadways, so give them some space so they can clear the roadways as quickly as possible. 
• Drive safely and don’t be our next customer!

WE WILL NEVER FORGET

Today, November 15th, marks 60 years since Officer Glenn W. Carlson paid the ultimate sacrifice in the line of duty. 

Earlier this year, we had the privilege of having members of Officer Carlson’s family at the CHP Truckee office for the dedication of a new, updated memorial for Officer Carlson. It was an honor meeting generations of Officer Carlson’s family and seeing his legacy live on. The California Highway Patrol is a family and we have promised to never forget those that laid down their life rather than swerve from the path of duty. Officer’s Carson’s bravery and sacrifice will never be forgotten. 

Officer Carlson was on duty when he stopped a speeding vehicle occupied by three men in a chain-controlled area on Donner Pass Rd. (Old Highway 40) west of Donner Lake in the Sierra Nevada. Officer Carlson issued the driver a citation.

Soon after, Officer Carlson was informed the car was stolen from a used car lot in Sacramento, and he began pursuit, according to CHP records. Unknown to the 33-year-old officer, those in the vehicle were fleeing from a Bank of America robbery hours earlier in Sacramento.

The vehicle pulled over in front of Loch Leven Lodge by Donner Lake, and the men — Robert Burns, a parolee from Oregon, and fellow parolees Roger Mealman and Clifford Toycen Jr. — exited and approached Carlson.

When Carlson ordered the suspects back, Mealman shot him five times with a 9 mm pistol, killing him. All three suspects were later apprehended and charged with Carlson's death.

In 2003 the SR-267 bypass in Truckee was dedicated in Officer Carlson's memory.

#Wewillneverforget #OfficerCarlson #chpfamily #chptruckee

Guess who’s starting to wake up after a long winter?? 🐻 With bear season in full effect in our area, here are some bear safety tips: 

1. When driving through the Truckee/Tahoe area, keep your eyes on the road ahead of you (High Visual Horizon) while scanning the environment for wildlife and make sure to buckle up!

2. Do not leave food in your car and lock your car doors!! 

3. If you encounter a bear face to face, stay calm and do not run! Walk away slowly from the bear and DO NOT push your slower friend down and use them as bait! Most bears just want to be left alone. 

Be Bear Aware out there! Here are more bear safety tips from National Park Service:  https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safety.htm

A STRONG SECOND!! According to UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab's (CSSL) latest update, we received approximately 737.2 inches (61 feet) of snow this winter season, making it the second snowiest season ever recorded at the CSSL (since 1946)!! By now, it’s safe to say that winter is finally over for us (we totally jinxed it) and we just want to thank all our surrounding agencies for all their hard work this winter while working under harsh, unforgiving conditions. ** WRONG WAY DRIVER ARRESTED ON I-80 OVER DONNER SUMMIT **

TRUCKEE, Calif. - On 03/15/2023, at approximately 12:15 pm, Officers from the California Highway Patrol’s (CHP) Truckee Area Office were dispatched to a call of a reckless driver, in the area of eastbound I-80, near the Donner Rest area. As Officers responded to the area, multiple calls were received by the Chico Communications Center advising a gold Lexus SUV was traveling wrong way, westbound, within the eastbound lanes of I-80. Allied agencies including Truckee Police and Nevada County Sheriff's Office also responded to assist with the incident. As the Lexus traveled wrong way within the eastbound lane of I-80, the Lexus was involved in a head on crash with a tractor trailer combination vehicle, resulting in minor injuries. Immediately after the crash had occurred, the driver of the Lexus attempted to flee the scene on foot. Witnesses on scene advised Chico Communications Center, the driver was jumping on passing vehicles and attempting to car jack vehicles from passing motorists. CHP Truckee officers arrived on scene, shortly after, and located the driver of the Lexus while he was fleeing on foot eastbound on I-80 near Castle Peak. CHP Truckee officers took the driver into custody, and he was subsequently booked into the Nevada County Jail for reckless driving, felony hit and run, suspicion of driving under the influence, and multiple other charges. 

The incident is still under investigation, if you have any information regarding this incident, please contact Officer Perez at (530) 563-9200.

**PRESS RELEASE/WEEKEND SKI TRAFFIC***

02/10/2023

Truckee, CA - In a continued effort to provide the highest level of safety and service to our community, CHP Truckee and Truckee PD are addressing safety concerns on Interstate 80 and through the town of Truckee, due to ski traffic, by conducting proactive traffic control on Saturdays and Sundays. 

What to expect:
Due to heavy visitation, expect delays along West River St. and State Route 89 between 7:00-9:00 AM (approximate).

Suggested route:
Access State Route 89 (south) by using Interstate 80 for ski traffic to Olympic Valley and the West Shore.

Tips:
Due to the unpredictable amount of heavy traffic, it is strongly advised to leave early and bring lots of patience.  If travel is necessary, the most advisable transportation method suggested is public transportation or carpooling.  TART is free to ALL riders and will deliver occupants closer to the mountain than any available parking. 

Impacts to operations:
Weather and unforeseen variables can hamper the effectiveness of traffic control measures.  Please consult with partners for up to the minute traffic and parking status (on Instagram at chp_truckee and FB at CHP-Truckee, www.palisadestahoe.com, https://quickmap.dot.ca.gov, https://tahoetruckeetransit.com )

59 years ago today, Officer Glenn W. Carlson laid down his life rather than swerve from the path of duty.

Officer Carlson was on duty when he stopped a speeding vehicle occupied by three men in a chain-controlled area on Donner Pass Rd. (Old Highway 40) west of Donner Lake in the Sierra Nevada.  Officer Carlson issued the driver a citation.

Soon after, Officer Carlson was informed the car was stolen from a used car lot in Sacramento, and he began pursuit, according to CHP records. Unknown to the 33-year-old officer, those in the vehicle were fleeing from a Bank of America robbery hours earlier in Sacramento.

The vehicle pulled over in front of Loch Leven Lodge by Donner Lake, and the men — Robert Burns, a parolee from Oregon, and fellow parolees Roger Mealman and Clifford Toycen Jr. — exited and approached Carlson.

When Carlson ordered the suspects back, Mealman shot him five times with a 9 mm pistol, killing him. All three suspects were later apprehended and charged with Carlson's death.

In 2003 the SR-267 bypass in Truckee was dedicated in Officer Carlson's memory.

Officer Carlson's sacrifice will never be forgotten here in Truckee.

PRESS RELEASE: FATAL TRAFFIC COLLISION INVOLVING A PEDESTRIAN

Carnelian Bay, Calif - On Monday, September 26, 2022, at approximately 10:44 pm, California Highway Patrol (CHP) Truckee Area officers received a call of a traffic collision on SR-28 at Sahara Rd. involving a vehicle and pedestrian.

The preliminary investigation shows a male resident from Tahoma, CA was driving a black 2021 Subaru Forester westbound on SR-28 from Agate Rd. The driver of the black Subaru Forester entered an active construction zone which was under one way traffic control. As the driver of the black Subaru approached the intersection of SR-28 at Sahara Dr., he failed to observe a traffic control worker in the roadway, instructing traffic to move from the westbound lane to the eastbound lane. Due to his level of intoxication, the driver of the black Subaru was unable to slow or avoid the highway worker standing in the roadway. The front of the black Subaru collided with the worker propelling the worker in a westerly direction on SR-28. The black Subaru continued westbound colliding with a dump truck and an excavator that were working at the job site. Due to the nature of the collision, the highway worker succumbed to their injuries and was pronounced deceased on scene. CHP Truckee units, with the assistance of the Placer County Sheriff’s department are handling the investigation. The driver of the black Subaru was subsequently arrested for suspicion of driving under the influence and was booked at the Nevada County Sheriff’s Station in Truckee. 
 
This traffic collision is still under investigation. If you have any information regarding this traffic collision, please contact Officer P. Mann at CHP Truckee (530) 563-9200.

TRAFFIC UPDATE:

SR-28 remains closed from Onyx St. to Agate Rd. due to a fatal traffic collision investigation.  There is no estimated time for reopening and please avoid the area if possible.

CHILD PASSENGER SAFETY WEEK

The California Highway Patrol Truckee Area along with Truckee Police will be conducting a child safety seat check-up event at the Truckee Lions Club Health Fair this Saturday, September 24th, 2022, from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. We will have Child Passenger Safety Technicians available to inspect car seats and the event will be on a first come first serve basis. 

Car crashes are sadly the leading cause of death for children 13 and under. According to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, 37% of the children killed in car crashes were unrestrained, meaning those deaths were preventable. Our goal is to educate parents/caregivers on proper installation and use of a child passenger seat to ensure that their most precious and vulnerable passengers are safe. 

#chptruckee #truckeepolice #ChildPassengerSafetyWeek #childseat #community #preciouscargo

August is Back-to-School Safety month and most schools have already started their school year however, kids in Nevada County and Placer County had their first day of school today and CHP Truckee, Truckee Police, and Placer County Sheriff's Office participated in a school enforcement operation focusing on violations including speeding and passing school buses. While we concentrated extra efforts today during back to school, enforcement will continue throughout the school year. 

Back-to-School means the return of increased pedestrian, crosswalk, and school zone traffic, so we would like to remind everyone to be extra mindful of increased activity near schools. Drivers should slow down and be alert in residential neighborhoods and school zones, put away all distractions, stop and yield to all school buses, and watch for children on and near the roadway. Parents should remind their children to use sidewalks when available, to cross only at marked crosswalks and to look both ways before doing so. Let’s help keep our children safe, together.

The California Highway Patrol is honored to celebrate the 20th anniversary of the AMBER Alert System and so very thankful for all the assistance from the public which has led to the successful recovery of 376 children. The California Highway Patrol (CHP) is celebrating the lifesaving collaboration of many partners behind the scenes, including members of the public, who have helped safely reunite hundreds of abducted children with their families during the 20 years of the state’s America’s Missing: Broadcasting Emergency Response (AMBER) Alert System. 

Here in Truckee, on April 16, 2020, the Truckee CHP office was notified through the Amber Alert System of a kidnapped and endangered 14-year-old girl out of Rupurt, Idaho. Utilizing the information received from the Amber Alert, Truckee CHP Officers were able to locate the suspected vehicle and apprehend one of the suspects. After further investigation and cell phone tracking, a search revealed the second suspect, and the victim were nearby in a wooded area covered with snow. Truckee CHP officers apprehended both suspects and were able to rescue the victim who was believed to be forced into human trafficking. Through the help of the California AMBER Alert Program and the combined commitment and resources from all involved agencies, we were able to make a potentially life-saving difference for this vulnerable girl and we were able to safely reunite her with her loved ones.

As the Amber Alert program has continued to evolve and throughout the years, the CHP has added secondary distribution methods to supplement the emergency alert system. These include the Wireless Emergency Alerts program, which is operated by the Federal Emergency Management Agency and the Federal Communications Commission; the California Department of Transportation’s Changeable Message Signs on freeways; @CHPAlerts on Twitter; and the California Lottery displays AMBER Alerts on digital terminal signs on lottery tickets.

The mission of the CHP is to Provide the highest level of Safety, Service, and Security. 

#chptruckee #CaliforniaHighwayPatrol #AmberAlert #20years #safetyservicesecurity

Good reminder to all who live up here and all that come to play up here, you are in Bear country and it was their home first!!!

Late last night CHP responded to assist Placer County Sheriff's Office with an extremely aggressive bear in the Northstar area. The bear had entered through an open window at an Air BnB and proceeded to destroy the house looking for food. The bear charged at Officers multiple times until they were able to get it back outside. The bear also had 3 cubs with it who were in a tree outside. 

The guest had locked themselves in rooms due to the aggressive nature of the bear. 

Just a reminder to be bear aware out there. 

Here are some good bear safety tips from the National Park Service 

https://www.nps.gov/subjects/bears/safety.htm#onthisPage-2

CHP AND ALLIED AGENCIES COMBINE EFFORTS TO KEEP ROADWAYS SAFE THIS HOLIDAY WEEKEND.

The ushering in of a new year brings with it the anticipation of a fresh start, positive changes, and healthy resolutions. What it should not bring are headlines of tragedies caused by drivers under the influence of drugs or alcohol.

To encourage safe travel for those who are out on the road, the California Highway Patrol (CHP) will conduct a Maximum Enforcement Period (MEP) starting at 6:01 p.m. on Friday, December 31, 2021, through 11:59 p.m. on Sunday, January 2, 2022.
“Ringing in the new year should be an exciting time filled with celebration and hope,” CHP Commissioner Amanda Ray said. “To help keep the roadways safe through the holiday and beyond, our officers will be out in force to deter, detect, and remove impaired drivers.”

During the previous New Year’s Day MEP, 56 people were killed in crashes in California. Sadly, half of the vehicle occupants killed in the crashes were not wearing a seat belt. During that same 78-hour MEP, CHP officers made 709 arrests for driving under the influence throughout the state.

To help bolster this year’s holiday traffic safety effort across state lines, the CHP will again partner with the National Highway Transportation Safety Administration and the Washington, Oregon, Idaho, Arizona, Nevada, Colorado, Utah, Montana, Wyoming, North Dakota, and South Dakota state patrols in a Western States Traffic Safety Coalition for the “Drive High, Get a DUI,” campaign. With the focus of the New Year’s operation to identify and remove impaired drivers from the road, the CHP will have all available personnel on patrol, including Drug Recognition Evaluators to conduct evaluations of suspected impaired drivers.

For daily MEP updates and other valuable traffic safety-related information, follow @CHP_HQ on Twitter.

The mission of the CHP is to provide the highest level of Safety, Service, and Security.

🎶🎵 It's beginning to look a lot like Christmas 🎶🎵 Lots of snow in the horizon starting tomorrow night all the way through the Christmas weekend. So PLAN AHEAD, and be ready for some winter driving conditions. ❄️❄️

During the last storm we received a lot of questions on what the different level of chain controls are in California during the winter season, so here is a little info to get you ready for the snow: 

- Requirement 1 (R-1): Chains are required on all vehicles except passenger vehicles and light-duty trucks under 6,000 pounds gross weight and equipped with snow tires on at least two drive wheels. Chains must be carried by vehicles using snow tires. All vehicles towing trailers must have chains on one drive axle. Trailers with brakes must have chains on at least one axle.

- Requirement 2 (R2): Chains or traction devices are required on all vehicles except four-wheel/all-wheel drive vehicles with snow-tread tires on all four wheels. NOTE: (Four-wheel/all-wheel drive vehicles must carry traction devices in chain control areas.)

- Requirement 3 (R3): Chains or traction devices are required on all vehicles, no exceptions.

R-1 and R-2 are the most common conditions. A highway will often be closed before an R-3 condition is imposed. Some local areas may use variations of these designations. You must follow the directions on the signs posted for chain controls or any instructions given by Caltrans or CHP personnel at chain control check points, even if these are at variance with broadcast road condition reports or information contained herein.

We get asked all the time why we can’t have trucks sleep in the chain on and off areas. 

The answer is two fold.

1: no one else can use the area to put on or take off chains. 

2: when the snow comes in, the sleeping trucks are now stuck and it takes hours to get them out to do snow removal. 

💫 the more you know 😂

It is with heavy hearts that we share the passing of Officer Reuben Warren, ID 17850, on December 8, 2021, due to complications from COVID-19.  

Officer Warren was 53 years old and proudly served his community as a CHP officer for more than 17 years.  He is survived by his wife, a son, and two daughters.

Prior to joining the CHP in 2005, he was an officer with the San Diego Police Department.  Officer Warren also served honorably as a United States Marine.

We get a lot of questions on what the different level of chain controls are in California during the winter season, so here is a little info to get you ready for the snow. R-1 and R-2 are the most common conditions. A highway will often be closed before an R-3 condition is imposed. Some local areas may use variations of these designations. You must follow the directions on the signs posted for chain controls or any instructions given by Caltrans or CHP personnel at chain control check points. Also, DO NOT install chains on your vehicle in the middle of the road as there are designated spots to install them...

For more information on winter driving tips: https://dot.ca.gov/travel/winter-driving-tips

58 years ago today, Officer Glenn W. Carlson laid down his life rather than swerve from the path of duty.

Officer Carlson was on duty when he stopped a speeding vehicle occupied by three men in a chain-controlled area on Donner Pass Rd. (Old Highway 40) west of Donner Lake in the Sierra Nevada.  Officer Carlson issued the driver a citation.

Soon after, Officer Carlson was informed the car was stolen from a used car lot in Sacramento, and he began pursuit, according to CHP records. Unknown to the 33-year-old officer, those in the vehicle were fleeing from a Bank of America robbery hours earlier in Sacramento.

The vehicle pulled over in front of Loch Leven Lodge by Donner Lake, and the men — Robert Burns, a parolee from Oregon, and fellow parolees Roger Mealman and Clifford Toycen Jr. — exited and approached Carlson.

When Carlson ordered the suspects back, Mealman shot him five times with a 9 mm pistol, killing him. All three suspects were later apprehended and charged with Carlson's death.

In 2003 the SR-267 bypass in Truckee was dedicated in Officer Carlson's memory.

Officer Carlson's sacrifice will never be forgotten here in Truckee.

The California Highway Patrol is currently accepting applications to take the test to become a Public Safety Dispatcher. This test is typically only given once a year in the Chico area. This is a very rewarding career with an excellent salary and benefits package to start. You can start this journey by going to CHPCareers.com, clicking the link to become a Public Safety Dispatcher and filling out an application to take the exam. The application filing period opened on April 23, 2021. Your initial exam application has to be postmarked and mailed no later than May 21, 2021. The test to become a Public Safety Dispatcher will be given at the CHP Chico Area Office tentatively the week of June 14, 2021. You must be 18 years of age to apply. Want a career that makes a difference? Don’t hesitate, apply today! If you have any questions or need assistance with this process, please don’t hesitate to call 530-332-1200 and ask to speak with a supervisor or Dispatch Supervisor II Vernon Mayer.

We are partnering with CHP – Golden Gate Division to participate in our "Saturation Saturday" event this coming Saturday, February 13th.   

This effort aims to reduce speeding and reckless driving as well as taking impaired drivers off the road along Interstate 80 from San Francisco, Calif. to the California/Nevada Stateline on Saturday, February 13, 2021, from 9:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.  This will be accomplished by enhancing traffic safety, enforcement, and education along this section of Interstate 80.  

This effort is intended to educate and if necessary use enforcement to help keep our roadways safe from these dangerous drivers.
We want to remind motorists to follow these basic traffic safety rules: always wear a seat belt, drive at a speed safe for conditions, eliminate distractions while driving, and always designate a sober driver.

As we get ready to welcome 2021 tonight, we would like to remind you that we will be out in full force for our annual New Year’s maximum enforcement period looking for impaired drivers. This year’s 78-hour period runs from 6 p.m. tonight, continuing through 11:59 p.m. on Sunday, January 3, 2021.

To help bolster its holiday traffic safety effort, the CHP will partner with the National Highway Transportation Safety Administration and the Washington, Oregon, Idaho, Arizona, Nevada, Montana, Wyoming, North Dakota, and South Dakota state patrols in a Western States Traffic Safety Coalition for the “Drive High, get a DUI,” campaign. With the focus of the New Year’s operation to identify and remove impaired drivers from the road, the CHP will ensure a contingent of its 579 Drug Recognition Evaluators are available to perform evaluations. 

“Heading into the new year, the mission of the CHP is unchanged to provide the highest level of Safety, Service, and Security,” CHP Commissioner Amanda Ray said. “Together, with the public’s commitment to safe and sober driving, our officers will continue to work to make California’s roadways safe for all who use them.” If you are celebrating this New Year’s Eve, don’t start off this new year in a tragedy and designate a sober driver ahead of time. Drive safe and Happy New Year!

#chptruckee #californiahighwaypatrol #nevadahighwaypatrol #happynewyear #designateadriver

Just some info for everyone, as we get a lot of questions on what the different level of chain controls are in California. R-1 and R-2 are the most common conditions. A highway will often be closed before an R-3 condition is imposed. Some local areas may use variations of these designations. You must follow the directions on the signs posted for chain controls or any instructions given by Caltrans or CHP personnel at chain control check points, even if these are at variance with broadcast road condition reports or information contained herein. 

https://dot.ca.gov/travel/winter-driving-tips/chain-controls

With heavy hearts we announce the End of Watch for Antelope Valley CHP Officer Andy Ornelas, ID 21685. 
 
On November 23, 2020, at approximately 7:05 pm, Officer Ornelas was riding his departmental motorcycle when a motorist pulled from the right shoulder to make a U-turn directly in his path. Officer Ornelas’ motorcycle struck the other vehicle, causing him to be thrown from his motorcycle. Officer Ornelas was transported to Antelope Valley Hospital in Lancaster.

Despite significant life-saving efforts by medical personnel, on Wednesday, December 2, 2020, Officer Ornelas succumbed to his injuries.  Officer Ornelas is survived by his wife, mother, father, brother, and sister. Officer Ornelas was a member of the Department for four years. 

Rest easy brother, we have the watch from here..

#officerornelas #21685 #EOW #restinpeacebrother #californiahighwaypatrol

Teen drivers can face challenges with every new experience. During National Teen Driver Safety Week, October 18-24, 2020, the California Highway Patrol (CHP) joins the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration to encourage parents and guardians to discuss driver safety with their young drivers. Motor vehicle crashes are the leading cause of death for teens in the United States, ahead of any type of injury, disease, or violence. According to preliminary data from the CHP’s Statewide 

Discussions about safe driving include avoiding distractions such as cell phones, using seat belts, obeying speed limits, not driving impaired, and limiting the number of passengers. Parents and guardians must be aware that their own driving behavior and following the same rules is as important as any conversation they may have with their teen. 

The CHP provides education to help keep teen drivers alive in its Start Smart course, offered by all CHP offices free of charge. Participating in Start Smart will help parents and guardians learn how to effectively discuss the importance of driving safely with their young drivers. Start Smart also teaches driver responsibility, the Graduated Driver License program, and collision- avoidance techniques. Due to the COVID-19 restrictions, Start Smart Classes held at the Truckee CHP Area office will be restricted to 10 participants and social distancing will be adhered to. All participants will be required to wear a face covering. Contact your local CHP Truckee Area office at (530) 563 -9200 for information on how to enroll.

Update:

Last night at approximately 11:22 pm, Truckee CHP units responded to a traffic collision on I-80 near Donner Pass Road.  Two juveniles were involved in a major injury solo vehicle collision resulting in the vehicle becoming engulfed in flames.  An off-duty Redwood City PD Officer happened upon the scene and was assisted by CHP Officer Jennings. They were able to pull the victims to safety while their vehicle burned.  HERO STATUS! Both juveniles were transported to the hospital via two air ambulances and we are hopeful of their recovery.  Details about the juveniles are not releasable but speed is suspected to be a factor in the collision.  No drugs or alcohol are suspected.

Press Release:

On April 16, 2020, the California Highway Patrol (CHP) Emergency Notification and Tactical Alert Center (ENTAC) received a call from the Rupert Police Department (RPD) in Idaho who related that at approximately 1900 hours (PDT) they received a call regarding a 14-year-old girl, Nayeli Ford (victim), who was taken by Sergio Alcantar (suspect #1) and Carmelo Galarza (suspect #2) driving a 2011 Gray Chevrolet Malibu (ID-2M77260). The investigation led RPD to believe the suspects were driving to California. RPD related that one of the suspects had a felony warrant in Idaho for sexual assault of a twelve-year-old. RPD believed the victim had been kidnapped and was in danger of being sexually assaulted or forced into human trafficking. They requested assistance with activating an AMBER Alert. ENTAC activated an AMBER Alert at 1938 hours, on behalf of Rupert PD along the Interstate 80 corridor. Cellphone tracking information showed the involved parties were possibly around the Truckee, CA area. At approximately 0004 hours, a CHP officer located the vehicle and one suspect near the Donner Summit Rest Area. Galarza was contacted in the driver seat of the vehicle.  After further investigation and the continued use of cell phone tracking information it was believed the victim and the second suspect were on foot and hiding in the area. Officers conducted a search of the area and located two sets of fresh footprints in the snow that led north into the forest. Alcantar and the victim were located 200 yards north of the rest area hiding behind a large tree in the snow. Galarza and Alcantar were placed in custody and transported to Nevada County Jail on kidnapping charges. Ford is in protective custody with CPS.

Just some info for everyone, as we all seem to forget what the different level of chain controls are in California.

Requirement 1 (R-1): Chains are required on all vehicles except passenger vehicles and light-duty trucks under 6,000 pounds gross weight and equipped with snow tires on at least two drive wheels. Chains must be carried by vehicles using snow tires. All vehicles towing trailers must have chains on one drive axle. Trailers with brakes must have chains on at least one axle.

Requirement 2 (R-2): Chains are required on all vehicles except four-wheel-drive vehicles under 6,500 pounds gross weight and equipped with snow tires on all four wheels. Chains for one set of drive wheels must be carried by four wheel-drive vehicles using snow tires.

Requirement 3 (R-3): Chains are required on all vehicles without exception.

R-1 and R-2 are the most common conditions. A highway will often be closed before an R-3 condition is imposed. Some local areas may use variations of these designations. You must follow the directions on the signs posted for chain controls or any instructions given by Caltrans or CHP personnel at chain control check points, even if these are at variance with broadcast road condition reports or information contained herein.

Winter isn't coming, it will be here tomorrow!!!  Meaning today is your last best travel day for the week!

Get ready for winter driving conditions and expect a slow go over the passes beginning tomorrow afternoon.  

If you haven't done so yet, prep your vehicle for winter.  Make sure your tires are in good shape and properly inflated, that you have windshield washer fluid, your windshield wipers are in good working order.  Also prep your winter kit, make sure you have water, and non perishable food in your car, have extra appropriate clothing, boots, gloves, hats blankets etc.. also make sure you have a cell phone charger and top off those fuel tanks and batteries before coming over the mountain!

With the very real possibility that utility companies may be shutting off the power due to extreme fire danger, it’s time to take a minute and go over a few things. 

Make sure your home is ready...
Flashlights 
Candles 
Food 
Batteries
Water 
And other necessities. 

Also when driving be extra cautious in areas as there will not be street lighting. Further, if you come to a controlled intersection that has lost power treat it as a four way stop. 

For those that don’t recall, (or choose not to remember) at a four way stop, always yield to the vehicle to your right if you arrive at the intersection at the same time. 

Be safe out there everyone.

Günter caught wind of the I-80 challenge and took a few days off from summer vacation to come out and help this 4th of July weekend. 

The California Highway Patrol’s (CHP) Valley Division and Golden Gate Divisions are partnering with the Nevada Highway Patrol (NHP) to participate in an “I-80 Challenge.”
 
This effort aims to reduce speeding drivers along Interstate 80 from San Francisco, Calif. to the Nevada/Utah Stateline from Wednesday, July 3, 2019 at 6:01 PM – Sunday, July 7, 2019 at 11:59 PM.  We will accomplish this by enhancing traffic safety, enforcement, and education along this section of the National Highway System.  The “I-80 Challenge” coincides with the CHP’s annual Independence Day Maximum Enforcement Period (MEP).
 
“Speeding endangers everyone on the road, speed limits are put in place to protect all road users,” said CHP Valley Division Chief Brent Newman. “We want to accomplish our goal of saving lives by educating motorists about the dangers of speeding and encouraging everyone to follow the rules of the road and drive safely.”
 
The CHP and NHP are asking motorists to accept the “I-80 Challenge” by promoting awareness and safe driving along the route.  During the “I-80 Challenge,” the CHP and NHP will increase traffic safety operations to educate and take appropriate enforcement action on drivers who violate traffic laws along Interstate 80.

We know its going to be hot down in the Sacramento Valley this week, but it will be warm here in the high country as well.

Here are some things to think about here in Truckee and Tahoe.  You are at 6,000 feet in elevation, so the sun can be extra intense, use Sunscreen.  Heat exhaustion can come on quickly even when it doesn't feel very hot outside.  Also with warm temps come increasing flows in the Truckee River, as well the water is still extremely cold.

Be careful out there!

This was a political cartoon and article about what was the State law of running Studded snow tires from December 1 to March 1, was published in the Tahoe World, October 21, 1975.

Public Service Announcement:

After what was a very busy weekend, just a quick reminder!

The Donner Pass Rest Areas are Rest Areas!  They are not nor have ever been snow play areas. There are frozen ponds behind the building that could be dangerous for would be snow players. 

Please search out other areas in the Truckee and North Tahoe Area, including but not limited to the Castle Peak Snow Play Area. 

Also there is a parking limit of 7 hours for a passenger vehicle inside the rest area. Do not use the rest area parking to access your favorite backcountry skiing destination. Your vehicle will be towed.

Wonder if these tires played a factor? 🤔🤷‍♂️

It’s January in Truckee and Tahoe. Tires like these do not meet tread requirements for chain control (6/32’s of tread) and by law are legally bald, (2/32’s of tread) 

Take the time and look over your vehicle. Make sure everything is in good working order before you come to play or work in the snow!

Get ready for Chain Control and Travel Delays as we head through the rest of the week.

Make sure you are winter ready before coming up the hill.  
-Have you put chains on your car before? (Practice in a nice warm garage before doing it for real in the wet and cold.
-Carry extra food and water
-Top off your gas tank, or battery if driving an EV and going over the hill.
-Have extra and proper clothing in your vehicle, including blankets, pants, jackets and gloves.
-charge your cell phone, its always dead when you need it most.
-Lastly SLOW DOWN its snowing!!!!

We have been getting a lot of questions about local events to help victims of the Camp Fire. 

Well Philosophy has this going on tomorrow Afternoon. All proceeds going to victims of the Camp Fire. 

Get out, support, enjoy and most importantly get home safe afterwards!!!

Just because it was snowing here didn’t mean we were done at the Camp Fire. 

This furry feline was found cold, wet and was suffering fro 2nd degree burns to all four of her paws. She did not protest her rescue and upon being wrapped in a blanket she immediately began to purr.  Once again our guys transported her to the emergency animal shelter located at the Chico airport.

This brave kitty was immediately set upon by an army of selfless volunteer’s  and veterinarians.  A very kind veterinarian, Dr Fox oversaw this cats medical care from beginning to end and at the end of the day Dr. Fox transported the cat to her own practice in Red Bluff so that she could personally see to the continued treatment and care of this cat.

Thanks to all the Veterinarians who continue to donate hundreds of hours helping all the animals affect by the Camp Fire.

As most of you know, CHP Truckee has had a majority of our office deployed to the devastating fires in Paradise, CA.  While on patrol, assisting locals and looking for suspected looters, we stumbled across this poor guy. He was still dutifully guarding the charred remains of his home. 

We quickly transported him to the makeshift animal rescue at the airport and were met with this selfless volunteer vet hailing from Oakland. She was volunteering tirelessly throughout the night and established an immediate bond with our pooch. This courageous guy survived 12 days up there by himself in the devastating rubble. What an incredible devoted surviver.

Our brothers and sisters down the highway at the weigh station (CHP – Donner Pass) had a little encounter the other night. 

We told them the Ice Cream machine was a bad idea!!!

As the guys head into week two of deployments to the Camp Fire from Truckee the destruction is just devastating. 

Our thoughts and prayers are with the people of Paradise and all the effected communities.

We have seen a lot of weird stuff in patrol cars but this might take the cake. 

The guys deployed up at the Camp Fire are out there making sure they can take care of any animals they come across.

Meet Hank the Hawk!  We got a call of a hurt bird on the freeway. As we arrived on scene and found Hank with a hurt leg and wing. 

We got Hank to the Bear League Tahoe. Now he is safe and sound and on his way to the vet!

Looking for an awesome time tomorrow night?

That’s right the game is on!!!

Truckee Police and CHP-Truckee have accepted the challenge and will be playing the Truckee High School boys varsity basketball team in a charity game tomorrow night!!!

Come one, come all and watch a bunch of old cops try and keep pace with the high scoring Truckee Wolverines. 

The game is at 6 PM tomorrow February 16th at Truckee High school.  Admission is just $5 with all proceeds going to the high school varsity basketball program. 

Can’t wait to see everyone there. Where your blue and gold and show support for your local old cops. 😂😂😂😂

Oopsie! If you are headed over the summit westbound on I-80 near Kingvale you might have a slight delay. The team is trying to get the road open ASAP. The slow lane will be closed until the magic happens. Make sure you have a full tank of patience!

Happy New Year Everyone,

Love Günter

Wow Truckee Love is for real. 

Save Mart came by with some really nice baskets and offered their condolences for the loss of Officer Andrew Camilleri. 

It’s actions like this that make us extremely proud to be Truckee’s Highway Patrol. 

Thanks guys. 

Officer Camilleri was tragically killed in the line of duty, on Christmas when his patrol vehicle was crashed into on I-880 in Hayward, CA.

Not the way we wanted to start the morning. 

Our thoughts and prayers are with our injured and Fallen Officer and their families this morning. 

Please pay attention while getting around this holiday season. We depend on it.

When Assistant Commissioner Silsbee calls, and informs you he needs Günter and his supervisors to @CHP Headquarters you hustle down there as quick as possible. 

The whole way down Günter was worried about what type of trouble he had gotten himself into. And with him leaving back to the North Pole later tonight he was very worried about what to say to Santa. 🎅 

However, the Commissioner had a surprise in store for the little guy. Commissioner Silsbee presented Günter with an award in “Superior Elfdom” and congratulated him on a terrific job during this busy holiday season. He went on to tell him he couldn’t wait to see him back on the highways next year. 

To say that this went to Günter’s Head is an understatement. I don’t think we will ever hear the end of it! 🤦‍♂️

Günter is out working a detail to help keep Caltrans District 3 safe today. 

Even got a little activity in. Got to keep that Sergeant happy. 

Remember to slow down and move over. Everyone wants to make it home this holiday season.

Günter had to hold over and cover the graveyard shift last night. 

Chipper and Günter hit the streets hard and even found them a DUI Rat who was up to some fishy business in North Lake. 

Solid work little buddy, the Sarge will be happy.

Günter was helping deliver presents today for CHIPs for Kids, with heavy lifting from Sierra Expeditionary Learning School.  

Together we helped dontate a huge amount of toys to Truckee Community Christmas in the hopes of making Christmas a little brighter for some local kids.

Günther is out keeping everyone safe working Chain Control today.  He even took time to answer some questions for the media regarding winter driving. 

Now I just need to teach him Facebook so I can retire! 😂🤔

Günter found himself in court this morning!

About a year ago, he was the lead investigator on an international milk and cookie theft ring. 

There were nine suspects arrested, including the ring leader, who had red cheeks and a jolly laugh! 🎄 🎅

Günter kept getting radio calls all morning and wasn’t answering. 

His partners were getting upset about having to handle his calls. 

We couldn’t figure out why he wasn’t handling his beat. Then we found him, stuffing his face like any good Cop!!!  

All was forgiven. The power of the 🍩 is something special.

Caltrans is alerting motorists that lane and highway closures on area freeways will be required to replace an overhead changeable message sign with new “Advanced Variable Message Sign” or AVMS. The new sign will be installed in the following location:

Interstate 80 westbound – Truckee – Donner Pass Road/Hwy 89

Sign Replacement will be tonight, Tuesday November 28th, from midnight to 4 a.m. Wednesday on westbound Interstate 80 in Truckee between Hwy 89 and the Donner Pass Road.

Motorists are encouraged to plan ahead for delays or use alternate routes. California Highway Patrol officers will be escorting traffic through the full closures using rolling blocks of traffic with full closures anticipated to be less than 30 minutes.

The new AVMS signs have increased energy efficiency, offer more options for messaging and offer higher visibility to provide motorists with real-time information for safe and efficient travel.

54 years ago tomorrow, Officer Glenn W. Carlson laid down his life rather than swerve from the path of duty.

Officer Carlson was on duty when he stopped a speeding vehicle occupied by three men in a chain-controlled area on Donner Pass Rd. (Old Highway 40) west of Donner Lake in the Sierra Nevada.  Officer Carlson issued the driver a citation.

Soon after, Officer Carlson was informed the car was stolen from a used car lot in Sacramento, and he began pursuit, according to CHP records. Unknown to the 33-year-old officer, those in the vehicle were fleeing from a Bank of America robbery hours earlier in Sacramento.

The vehicle pulled over in front of Loch Leven Lodge by Donner Lake, and the men — Robert Burns, a parolee from Oregon, and fellow parolees Roger Mealman and Clifford Toycen Jr. — exited and approached Carlson.

When Carlson ordered the suspects back, Mealman shot him five times with a 9 mm pistol, killing him. All three suspects were later apprehended and charged with Carlson's death.

In 2003 the SR-267 bypass in Truckee was dedicated in Officer Carlson's memory.

Officer Carlson's sacrifice will never be forgotten here in Truckee.

In light of multiple reports in the Tahoe, Reno, and Sacramento area we thought this would be a good opportunity to remind people of the dangers of human trafficking and some simple steps to help keep you and your loved ones safe. 

Curtesy of Reno PD:

Be alert and aware of your surroundings at all times.

Trust your instincts. If you don’t feel safe, find ways to make it safer or leave.

Have your cell phone with you.

Keep your children close to you in public.

Educate your children about stranger danger.

If you believe somebody may be following you on foot, move to a public area and contact police with any overtly suspicious activity. If you believe someone may be following your vehicle, vary your route and driving pattern in an attempt to avoid the vehicle. If the vehicle persists in following you, drive to a safe destination such as a police department or the fire department and call police.

Every year at this time we se an uptick in completely preventable traffic collisions, as the mass migration to Black Rock City happens. 

Make sure your vehicle is road worthy prior to setting off for Black Rock City. 

Ensure you are not over GVWR, make sure tires are properly inflated, ensure that everything is strapped down and secured to your vehicle, and remember 55 mph is the Maximum speed limit when towing in California. 

Can't have any fun at Burning Man if you never make it in the first place.

Are you over the age of 55?  Looking for something fun and rewarding to do?  Always curious what it would be like to work with the CHP?

Well CHP Truckee is looking to add to our Senior Volunteer Program.

Senior Volunteer Program 

• Senior citizens who are interested in justice and law enforcement are encouraged to volunteer for the Senior Volunteer Program (SVP) of the CHP. 

• Motivated citizens who want to contribute to their community by supporting public safety, service, and security may be ideal candidates. The program enables qualified individuals to lend their knowledge, skills, talents and wisdom to the CHP. 

• The SVP contributes greatly to the Department’s community outreach, as well as enhancing operational efficiency and public service. The SVP enables CHP officers to provide more public service. 

• Volunteers must: 
-Be age 55 or older 
-Be able to work at least four hours a week 
-Be available to work during regular business hours 
-Have a clean driving record and no felony convictions 
-Be able to pass a background check, including at least two interviews, fingerprinting, and review by the Department of Justice and Federal Bureau of Investigation. 
-Complete the CHP Senior Volunteer Training Program. 
-Sign a Statement of Professional Ethics. 
-Senior Volunteers are also responsible for their own uniforms (white shirt, navy blue slacks, and proper badge). 
 
• SVP duties fall into four general categories: administration, public affairs, volunteer use of CHP vehicles, and ride-alongs with uniformed officers. Duties vary depending on the needs of each command and the preferences and skills of each volunteer. 

- Tasks include administrative duties, radar trailer deployment, assisting with traffic control, participating in community outreach events, child safety seat installation, and serving as school crossing guards. 

• The Senior Volunteer Program has won the 2015 “Outstanding Achievement in Law Enforcement Volunteer Programs” Award from the International Association of Chiefs of Police. Other awards to the SVP and individual members include the Commissioners’ Unit Citation and the California Peace Officers Association Award of Distinction. 

If you would like more information or would like to join, contact Officer Lack at 530-582-7570

It is with extremely heavy hearts that CHP Truckee reports the passing of a beloved member of our community. Coach Shaffer was killed and his family injured last night as a result of a head on traffic collision outside of Sierraville. 

Coach Shaffer was a pillar of our small community. He was the Head Coach who led the fabled Truckee Wolverines to a 4-peat State Championship. Many of our own kids played for Coach Shaffer over the years and as a community this is a extremely tough loss. 

Please keep the Shaffer family in your thoughts and prayers. 

RIP Coach 

CHP - Quincy will be handling this collision investigation to its conclusion.

Ever wonder what it takes to be a school bus driver?  Or wonder who these hard working people are who drive our kids around?

A school bus driver must complete 20 hrs of class room training, 20 hrs of behind the wheel training, obtain commercial license and pass a background check. Also they must pass first aid, and a rules/regulations test with the California Highway Patrol. They also must complete a pre -trip test and a behind the wheel test with the CHP. Once these tests are completed and all training verified, the CHP will provide driver with a special drivers certificate. 

This process has to repeated every 5 yrs to keep driving. 

Pic of a CHP Senior Volunteerwith Tahoe Truckee Unified School Districts most recent school bus driver renewal.  Congratulations!

A message to all of you from our Commander!

On behalf of the CHP Truckee Area office, I would like to take a moment to express our sincerest appreciation to the Truckee/North Tahoe Communities and those who support us.  This includes those who utilize Interstate 80 frequently and who enjoy following us. 

I would be remiss if I did not thank our incredible first responder partners who collectively make our communities much safer.  Most especially our friends at the Placer County Sheriff's Office and the Truckee Police Department.

We have been through tremendous challenges through the last few years.  Most traumatic was the loss of one of our own within the Truckee Area last year.  We received incredible local community support which helped carry us through.

Together we have faced incredible natural disasters to include, but not limited to, forest fires, floods, epic snow levels, rock slides, mud slides, avalanches, major collisions, Ironman Triathlons and even serious crime on occasion.   Together we have experienced incredible tourism growth, growing communities/resorts and limitations to our infrastructure.          

The CHP Truckee Area has nearly 160,000 Facebook followers (more than any other CHP local office in the State of California) who look to the CHP Truckee Facebook page on a regular basis.  Media outlets from around the country and even internationally, at times, follow us for current roadway conditions, weather conditions and a little humor, mixed with a true blend of our gregarious CHP Truckee personality.  

On behalf of the entire CHP Truckee Area Office, I would like to express our gratitude for your continued support of us, which has truly been like nowhere else.  It means more to us than you will ever know.  We know that our success is based on our community support.

We are fortunate, honored, and humbled to serve this community of ours, as well as those who travel through it.

-Captain Ryan Stonebraker, CHP Truckee Area Commander

As most of you heard we tragically lost Officer Chellew last week as he was riding his CHP Motorcyle while in a pursuit.

We have had a lot of inquires from everyone out there on how they can help, well here is how you can:

C.A.H.P CREDIT UNION ESTABLISHES MEMORIAL FUND FOR FALLEN CHP OFFICER LUCAS FORREST CHELLEW

SACRAMENTO, CA- The C.A.H.P Credit Union has established a memorial fund in honor of fallen CHP Officer Lucas Forrest Chellew.  One hundred percent of all donations received will go directly to Officer Chellews’s family.  The C.A.H.P Credit Union is covering all processing fees and administrative responsibilities.

On February 22, 2017, CHP Officer Lucas Forrest Chellew was involved in a collision during a pursuit in South Sacramento. Officer Chellew lost control of his motorcycle, after being cut off by another vehicle, and crashed into a pole.  

Officer Chellew was transported to UC Davis Medical Center. Despite best efforts from the medical staff, Officer Chellew passed away shortly after arriving at the hospital. He leaves behind a wife, son and daughter. His father is retired CHP Captain Charles Chellew, and his sister is CHP Officer Hanna Chellew.   

“We are deeply saddened by the loss of Officer Chellew,” said Brad Houle, C.A.H.P. Credit Union President.   “He was an exemplary officer and dedicated public servant.  Our hearts mourn for his family left behind and he will be greatly missed by his fellow officers and the community he loved to serve.”  

Donations can be made on the C.A.H.P Credit Union website (www.cahpcu.org) or directly mailed to:

Lucas Forrest Chellew Memorial Fund
CAHP Credit Union
2843 Manlove Road
P.O. Box 276507
Sacramento, CA 95827-6507
